lat: vir ille summus nullus imperator fuit
eng: that great man was no general at all
alternatives: that supreme man was no commander at all
that highest of men was no general at all
note: Nullus is the Latin emphatic negation of identity --- not `not at all a' but `no general at all'. Rendering with `no ... at all' preserves the bite. The line is among the most famous Cicero ever wrote on Pompey.

lat: ubi non sis qui fueris, non esse cur velis vivere
eng: when you are not the man you were, there is no reason to want to live
alternatives: when you are not what you used to be, there is no reason to wish to live
note: Cicero calls this an `old saying' (vetus est enim); he likely has a Greek tag in mind, perhaps from comedy. Rendering directly preserves the doubled `you / you' that makes the line memorable.

eng: in the power of our friends
alternatives: in the hands of our friends
in the control of our friends
note: Friends here is the standard Ciceronian euphemism for the triumvirs after Luca. Power preserves the political-control register that hands or control would soften.

lat: tenent omnia idque ita omnis intellegere volunt
eng: they hold everything, and they want everyone to know it
alternatives: they have the whole thing in hand, and they want all to understand that
note: The point is the doubled tenent / volunt: not just dominance but the visible display of dominance. The English shortens to keep the punch.

eng: scornful of Syria, blustering about Spain
note: The two participles balanced by tense and tone: Pompey speaks of his rival's province with feigned contempt, of his own with hollow swagger. `Blustering' (for iactans) catches the hollowness Cicero is signalling.

lat: in illa tua sedecula quam habes sub imagine Aristotelis
eng: in that little chair of yours that you have under the bust of Aristotle
alternatives: in that little seat of yours under the image of Aristotle
note: Sedecula is the diminutive of sedes; Atticus's reading-chair, located under the imago (likely a herm or bust) of Aristotle, is one of the most cherished domestic images in the corpus. `Bust' for imago because the English `image' loses the visual specificity.

eng: on condition the elections actually hold
alternatives: if the elections actually take place
note: ut sit rata is parenthetical: a conditional wish that the comitia actually be ratified. Cicero is not sorry to have missed the senate's altercations — provided the elections do happen and his absence is not a forfeit. Reading follows Shackleton Bailey.

tēn parousan is feminine accusative singular: "the (lady) present." Conventional reading: Pilia, Atticus's wife. Cicero asks that Atticus arrange for Pilia to be at home so he does not arrive as a stranger. The Greek is the discreet hint.

eng: many flashes of genius, yet much craft as well
alternatives: many flashes of genius, yet of no great craft
note: Textual crux: Perseus carries inultae (corrupt). Watt OCT prints multae tamen artis = "yet of much craft also"; Shackleton Bailey prefers non multae tamen artis = "yet of no great craft." The two readings carry opposite verdicts on Lucretius. Going with the OCT reading as the safer general consensus and the more probably correct sense (Cicero is praising both genius and craft, with tamen marking the pair).

lat: virum te putabo si Sallusti Empedoclea legeris, hominem non putabo
eng: I shall count you a man if you read Sallustius's Empedoclea; a human being I shall not count you
alternatives: I shall reckon you a hero for reading it, but no human being
note: The pun is vir / homo: superhuman in stamina (vir), but inhuman in feeling (non homo). The English keeps the two words distinct.

essedum is the British and Gallic two-horse light war-chariot, much remarked on by Caesar's officers in De Bello Gallico 4. Cicero's joke: if Britain has no plunder, Trebatius can ride one of those home. "War-chariot" preserves the Gallic-British specificity "chariot" alone would lose.

eng: you are urging on a runner
alternatives: you are urging on a man already running
note: Standard Latin idiom: to encourage someone already moving in the desired direction. The English keeps the metaphor intact for the long horse-and-chariot extension that follows.

eng: softer than the lowest little ear-tip
alternatives: softer than the very tip of an ear-lobe
note: oricula is the diminutive of oris (auris): the little ear, the ear-tip; oricula infima the lowest, softest part. A Roman household proverb for extreme pliancy. Keeping the diminutive ("little ear-tip") preserves the affectionate exaggeration.

Perseus prints "eximus iudiciis," plainly a typographic corruption. Reading eximiis (so Watt OCT, Shackleton Bailey): Caesar's outstanding/exceptional judgements about young Crassus, conveyed (perhaps in correspondence) and known to Cicero.

litterae Graecae here means Greek-language writing/literature, not just Greek script: Apollonius proposes a prose history of Caesar's wars in the Hellenistic-celebratory tradition (cf. Theophanes of Mytilene on Pompey).

alternatives: I have you on my account
note: The idiom takes its image from the account-ledger (aes); a modern English idiom that keeps the bookkeeping image is preferable to a paraphrase like "in my circle." "On my books" reads cleanly and preserves the ledger metaphor that Cicero builds on with magno cumulo ("with a great heap") at the close.

eng: little eyes of Italy, my own small villas
note: The diminutives 'little eyes of Italy, my own small villas' carry the Catullan Sirmio, insularum ocelle as a probable allusion --- the bay-of-Naples estate-talk drawing on the Sirmio territory of intimate ownership. Worth a high-certainty allusion-sidecar entry.

The Leucopetra turnabout as the corpus's clearest first-person account of the decision that produces the Philippics. The letter combines the storm-driven return, the Velia meeting with Brutus, and the Olympia-reproach insinuation into a single narrative arc; the implicit answer to the o Brute, ubi es? apostrophe in 16.8. Worth a sustained corpus-level translator-note on the Velia-meeting hinge.

alternatives: not such as to draw nemesis
note: A markedly epic-register Greek choice for a letter whose subject is whether his return from Leucopetra was honourable or shameful. Worth a translator-note on Cicero's reach for Homer when the question is his own honour.

The antiquarian apostrophus numeral form is preserved in Perseus and rendered 'three thousand veterans' in the English. Worth a glossary-realia note on the apostrophus numeral system and its survival in epistolary Latin.

eng: on the proper duties... I dedicate
note: The double-hinge letter: 'on the proper duties... I dedicate' marks De Officiis in active dedication, while nostrum opus 'my work' circulating among Sextus Peducaeus's circle marks the Second Philippic in pre-publication pamphlet form. Worth a sustained corpus-level note on these two parallel literary projects through November 44.

eng: One man is to me ten thousand
note: Heraclitus B49 --- the philosophical-justification fragment Cicero deploys to make his political stance bear weight. High-certainty allusion-sidecar entry candidate for the Heraclitus fragment.

lat: dolichon ploon hormainonta, par' enemoenta Mimanta
note: Two phrases from Odyssey 3.169--172 in the same letter --- Cicero's epistolary citation habit at its densest. Worth a translator-note on Cicero's distinctive Homeric mosaic-citation style and an allusion-sidecar entry for both.

lat: avi tui pronepos ... aedem Opis explicaturum
eng: your grandfather's great-grandson writes to my father's grandson... will open the temple of Ops
note: The Roman-family identification that was transparent to Atticus is now opaque; preserved literally with a flag in the headnote. Worth a translator-note on the unrecoverable referent.

eng: may I never be saved by the likes of him
note: Outburst against Octavian. Bitterly ironic given the Second Triumvirate proscriptions a year later that proscribe and kill Cicero. Worth the most sustained translator-note in the cluster on Cicero's missed reading of the young heir.
The obscure comic-stage type (perhaps from a lost Plautine play) applied to Antony as a strutting-soldier caricature. Worth a glossary-realia note on the Stratullax type and its application.

Perseus-TEI truncation versus modern composite editions. A corpus-level note on the discrepancy between Perseus's brief cover-note and the standard Shackleton Bailey / Tyrrell--Purser composite of 16.16 + 16.16A--E. Worth flagging in the manifest so the reader of the bound volume sees the editorial scope.

lat: he deur' hodos soi ti dynatai nyn, theoprope?
eng: what does this journey of yours hither avail you now, prophet?
note: Euripides, Iphigeneia in Aulis 956 self-redeployment. The line Achilles flings at Calchas the prophet --- 'what does this journey of yours hither avail you now?' --- Cicero turns into a self-question after the council of Antium fails to clarify Brutus's course. Worth a corpus-level translator-note both for the Euripidean identification and for the move from prophet-figure to political-actor-figure.

Cicero's first substantial portrait of Octavian's schooling under the Caesarian veterans deploys katechesei --- a markedly Greek-philosophical word for 'indoctrination / oral instruction' usually applied to Stoic or Epicurean catechesis. Cicero is reading the young heir as a philosophical-pupil-in-formation; the political implications are sharp and the word-choice doctrinal. Worth a sustained corpus-level translator-note.

lat: philosophoumen ta peri tou kathekontos. prosphonoumen
eng: we philosophize the proper duties. we dedicate
note: De Officiis-in-composition announcement. The October-25 dating places this letter four months later than the surrounding June cluster, and the line is the first explicit notice of De Officiis under composition --- a corpus-level hinge.

lat: di illi mortuo qui umquam Buthrotum!
note: The elliptical imprecation against 'whichever dead man' first proposed the Buthrotian land seizure preserves the depth of Cicero's frustration with the veteran-settler programme; the referent (likely a Caesarian veteran or settler) is unrecoverable. Worth a translator-note on the ellipsis and a glossary note on the Buthrotum issue.

High-certainty allusion to Iliad 16.779 / Odyssey 9.58 ('at the ox-loosing,' i.e. late afternoon when oxen are unyoked) --- the more colloquial of Cicero's Homeric phrase-borrowings. Worth a future allusion-sidecar entry.

The Iulius-Quinctilis joke 'could anything be more disgraceful than July for Brutus?' The renaming of Quinctilis (the fifth month) to Iulius after Caesar makes the proper time-marking of Brutus's ludi Apollinares into a political offence; holding the games in 'July' rather than Quinctilis silently endorses the Caesarian-calendar rebrand. Worth a sustained translator-note both on the punning sharpness and on the corpus-wide question of how Cicero handles month-names in post-Ides letters.

lat: dum modo doleant aliquid, doleant quidlibet
eng: as long as they suffer something, let them suffer whatever
note: Quasi-versified close. The metrically-accented phrasing in Perseus suggests a versified citation, possibly from Naevius or Accius, but unidentified. Worth flagging as an open allusion-sidecar candidate.

Ennian tag. The high-certainty Ennius Annales tag turns on Atticus's praenomen Titus --- 'O Titus, if anything...' The vocative play between epic-fragment and personal address is one of Cicero's recurring Ennian moves; worth a corpus-level note on the recurrence pattern.

Homeric re-pointing (logoio for gamoio). Cicero's substitution of logoio ('of speech') for the Homeric gamoio ('of marriage') in Iliad 5.428--429 --- Aphrodite's instruction to Helen to leave the works of war for the works of marriage becomes Cicero's self-instruction to leave the works of war for the works of speech --- is one of the corpus's sharpest single Homeric reapplications and a doctrinal hinge for the April-44 letters' negotiation of the post-Ides political withdrawal.

eng: from the rock --- to the cross
note: The Dolabella-and-the-pyre-altar celebration as out-of-order chronology. The K. Mart. = 1 March 44 BC dating, two weeks before the Ides, sits the letter chronologically before the Ides cluster but in manuscript order after the late-April letters because of the manuscript transmission. The de saxo, in crucem asyndeton compresses the Tarpeian Rock and crucifixion into a single tight construction --- the punitive register Dolabella adopted against the false-Marius pretenders, and one of the clearest single windows onto Cicero's brief Dolabellan enthusiasm before the April-May letters' disillusionment.

Reference as a corpus-level question. The identity of the suppressed political work referenced as anekdoton --- debated between De Gloria and the lost Secret History --- is a sustained scholarly crux. Worth a corpus-level note when the apparatus pass runs.

lat: minore periculo ... vivo tyranno dici potuisse quam mortuo
eng: things could be said less dangerously with the tyrant alive than with him dead
note: Political-register line. One of the cluster's sharpest single post-Ides political observations and a candidate for the volume's chosen pull-quote for the April-44 sequence.

lat: hoc metuere, alterum in metu non ponere
note: A rhythmical iambic-feel line in the manuscripts (some editors print it with metrical accents), possibly Cicero's own off-the-cuff iambics rather than a quotation. Worth a sustained translator-notes entry on the quotation-vs.-own-coinage question.

lat: allois en esthlois tond' apothountai psogon
eng: in other goods men shake off this reproach
note: Sophoclean fragment. Attributed by editors to the lost Sophoclean Erigone; rendered as a tidy English clause inside quotation marks. Worth a future allusion-sidecar entry when that pass runs.

lat: me Idus Martiae non delectant. ... excisa enim est arbor, non evulsa
eng: the Ides of March do not delight me ... for the tree was cut down, not torn up by the roots
note: Retraction as the corpus's sharpest single Ides-of-March recantation. The tree-image antithesis (cut down vs. torn up by the roots) is the doctrinal hinge of Cicero's May-44 disillusionment with the assassination's incompleteness; the rendering 'the tree was cut down, not torn up by the roots' preserves the violent extraction the verb evellere insists on. Worth a sustained corpus-level translator-note both for the rhetorical figure and for the historical pivot.

The Hellenistic-comic proverb (the tale of digging up a buried treasure to find only ashes) is mordantly redeployed for Cassius's request that Cicero use his influence with Hirtius. Worth a translator-note on the proverb's Aesopian / New-Comedy provenance and Cicero's choice to wield it in Greek rather than render it in Latin.

Embedded sub-letter. One of the rare passages in the entire correspondence where Hirtius's voice survives verbatim; the vesperi timestamp gives the May-27 evening more granular detail than usual. Worth a corpus-level note for the doctrinal weight of Hirtius's words as transmitted by Cicero.

Formula as the Servius-jab's load-bearing detail. The technical opening-of-a-property-suit formula has to be heard as the opening move, not the substantive proceeding, for the Servius jab 'not in the formula but in what follows from it' to land. Worth a glossary note on the formula and a separate translator-note on the rendering.

eng: a piece of hospitality --- a billeting, rather --- that was a burden to me, not a bother
note: The Caesar-at-Puteoli letter as a corpus-level historical pivot. The Latin hospitium/Greek epistathmeia pivot is the sharpest single political-register line in the 45 BC cluster --- the moment Cicero codes Caesar's visit as not peer guest-friendship but military quartering. Worth a sustained corpus-level note both for the rhetorical move and for the historical occasion (the only surviving first-person record of Caesar dining with Cicero). The exclamatory 'Oh, what a burdensome guest he was --- and yet I have no regrets' is chosen over the older 'burdensome but not regrettable' on exclamatory-accusative grounds.

lat: nihil perditius; explicari rem non posse; ille gaudens
eng: Nothing could be more lost; the thing cannot be untangled; he said it, at any rate, with pleasure
note: The Matius 'everything is in confusion' opener as the corpus's threshold to the post-Ides world. The three-hammer trimeter-feel is the cluster's clearest single window onto the immediate political mood. Worth a corpus-level note both for the rhetorical construction and for what Cicero's ear hears in a Caesarian's mouth on 7 April 44 BC.

eng: the tyranny lives, the tyrant fallen
note: A two-clause four-word chiasm that distinguishes the death of the tyrant from the survival of the tyranny --- one of the sharpest single rhetorical figures in the whole post-Ides cluster, and the doctrinal hinge of Cicero's despair through April 44 BC. Worth a sustained translator-note on the rendering 'the tyranny lives, the tyrant fallen.'

eng: my mind is divided on speaking the plain truth
note: The Pindar Nemean 7.25 tag --- one of the densest single-letter Pindaric allusions in the corpus. Worth flagging in a future allusions-pass.

The currentem tu quidem idiom and the Pollex/index pun. Two diagnostic short-letter idiom cruxes worth recording: currentem hortari = 'urge on a runner' (13.45); Pollex/index thumb-pun (13.46). Both are the sort of micro-decision the project's translator-notes apparatus exists to capture.

The Vatinius supplicatio campaign (Fam. 5.9 + 5.11) as a structural exception. 5.9 (incoming from Vatinius) and 5.11 (Cicero's outgoing reply) bracket Vatinius's mid-45-BC angling for a supplicatio honouring his Illyrian campaign; the honor/periculum pun (5.9 section 1) is the structural hinge.

The post-Tullia Lucceius exchange (Fam. 5.13--5.14) as a two-way consolation cluster. 5.14 from Lucceius (incoming, 9 May 45 BC) and 5.13 from Cicero (outgoing reply, mid-March 45 BC --- the manuscript-order is not chronological) form an exchange in the Servius Sulpicius mode but with Lucceius as the addressee; the praeceptorem fortitudinis 'teacher in bravery' line in 5.13 is the doctrinal hinge.

The 'letter to Caesar' withdrawal as a structural pivot in the cluster. The Greek problema Archidemou (Stoic-dialectical 'Archidemus's puzzle') frames the dilemma; the bitter hunc de pompa Quirini contubernalem (Caesar as 'messmate of Quirinus's procession,' i.e. his statue carried with the gods at the ludi) is the cluster's sharpest single line of political register. Worth a corpus-level note as the moment Cicero finally abandons the project of writing an advice-letter to Caesar.

eng: a sop to balance my Cato
note: The Cato/Anticato sop-register. The Catonis meiligma 'a sop to balance my Cato' is the cluster's clearest single window onto how Cicero received Caesar's Anticato as a polemic exchange. The epiteugma/apoteugma pair (success/failure) is also worth a glossary note as the Stoic-rhetorical technical vocabulary the letter deploys.

The Dicaearchus revival cluster (Att. 13.30, 13.31, 13.32, 13.33). Dicaearchus's Descent (katabaseos), On the Soul (peri psyches), and the Tripolitikon surface across four consecutive letters as the framing models for Cicero's own projected dialogue politikon syllogon ('political assembly'). The cluster is the densest single deployment of Dicaearchus in the corpus and is worth a sustained allusion-pass entry.

lat: tibi gratulor, mihi gaudeo; te amo, tua tueor; a te amari et quid agas quidque agatur certior fieri volo
note: The Fam. 6.15 chiasm as a single-sentence masterpiece. The Ides-of-March note to Basilus is six clauses of perfect I/you-interlocking parallelism, and the deniable passive quid agatur ('what is being done') is itself an act of literary self-protection. Worth a corpus-level note both for the rhetorical construction and for the date.

The Bithynicus exchange (Fam. 6.16--6.17) as the cluster's unusual two-way pair. 6.16 is the only incoming letter in book 6's post-Pharsalus cluster; 6.17 is Cicero's direct reply. Worth registering as a structural exception to the cluster's one-direction-only norm. The beneficiorum magnitudine vs. necessitudine antithesis is the doctrinal hinge of 6.17.

The Arpinum daily-letter Academica-shuffle cluster (Att. 13.10--13.25). The cluster's binding preoccupation is the rapid-cycle revision of the Academica: dedication shifted from Catulus/Lucullus to Varro (13.12, 13.13, 13.16, 13.19, 13.25), book-count expanded from two to four (13.13), and the prefatory prosphonesis to Varro composed and revised across multiple letters. The Greek Akademiken syntaxin 'the Academic arrangement' (13.16, 13.19) becomes a fixed cluster keyword. Worth a corpus-level note as the first sustained look at a Ciceronian philosophical publication in active revision.

The symbiosis register in Att. 13.23. symbiosis 'shared life' is the cluster's most resonant Greek term for the Atticus friendship under strain --- the veiled grievance against Brutus (whose recent behaviour provokes the remark) is left unspoken in the Latin. Worth flagging as a binding emotional keyword of the Atticus correspondence in this stretch.

eng: the wise man will speak openly
note: The Paetus parrhesia letter (Fam. 9.22) and its place in the Paetus cluster. The bawdy Stoic-parrhesia set-piece (the elaborate defence of plain Latin terms for genital matters: mentula, coleos, cauda, penis, the corrupt stage-quotations and the false etymologies) is the cluster's most distinctive single performance. The Greek ho sophos euthyrrhemonesei 'the wise man will speak openly' frames the whole as Stoic doctrine; the joke turns on Cicero adopting the philosophical pretext for what is in fact sustained low-diction comedy. The suppedit reading 'pisses underneath himself' vs. Shackleton Bailey's emendation is the translator-note candidate.

eng: the consciousness of one's deeds and counsels
note: The Pompeian-exile doctrinal pivot. The same Stoic-consolatio formula recurs verbatim in Fam. 6.10.4 and 6.13.5 --- a deliberate binding refrain across the Book 6 cluster. Worth flagging as the cluster's stable doctrinal pivot; the 6.12.5 sharpening to 'now even of preservation [nunc vero etiam salutem]' is the cluster's most acute formulation.

Homer and Hesiod allusions in Atticus 13.x. Iliad VI.442 (Hector's aideomai Troas) at 13.13; Iliad 11.654 (tacha ken kai anaition aitiooto) at 13.25; Hesiod Works and Days 350 (ai ke dunai) at 13.12. Worth corpus-level allusion entries and glossary notes at the apparatus pass.

The Paetus voice register reactivated (Fam. 9.15--9.21). After the long gap since 9.10--9.13 was drafted in earlier sessions, the Paetus cluster's distinctive register returns in force: food language as Cicero's medium of self-mockery (tyrotarichum, lucanicae, polypus miniati Iovis, the appetizer-to-roast-veal promulside ad assum vitulinum), chiastic epigrams (dicendi discipulos, cenandi magistros; salis satis est, sannionum parum), deliberate slumming into low diction (the Popillius/denarius cruxes in 9.16, the threat-as-affection close bona tua comedim in 9.20), mock-formal verbs (censeo), and the wordplay that drives each joke's whole point (Balbi/balbos in 9.19; the philosophical defection-to-Epicurus military metaphor in 9.20). Worth a corpus-level note as the cluster's binding voice across the 18 months it spans.

lat: conscientiam rectae voluntatis maximam consolationem esse rerum incommodarum
note: The Torquatus consolation register vs. the Toranius condolence register (Fam. 6.2--6.4 vs. 6.21). Two Pompeian-exile registers in proximity: Torquatus letters are grave, philosophically pitched, with full Stoic-consolatio machinery deployed (conscientiam rectae voluntatis maximam consolationem esse rerum incommodarum as the doctrinal pivot); Toranius letter is more distant, more measured, with the philosophical resources surfacing only briefly. The two registers should sound audibly different even though the subject (Pompeian defeat, Caesarian clemency, Athens-as-refuge) is the same.

eng: but in front of excellence [the gods set sweat]
note: The Hesiod quotation in Fam. 6.18. The Hesiod Works and Days 289 quotation tes d' aretes hidrota ``but in front of excellence [the gods set sweat]'' is a canonical line, frequently quoted by Cicero across his philosophical and rhetorical works. Worth a corpus-level allusion entry and a glossary note when the apparatus pass reaches Fam. 6.

The Caesarian municipal-law technical distinction in Fam. 6.18.1. The qui faciunt / qui fecissent contrast (currently practising auctioneers barred from public office vs. former ones not barred) is a small but precise window onto the legal-technical drafting style of Caesar's lex municipalis. Worth preserving literally in translation; modernizing into ``current'' / ``former'' loses the explicit verbal-aspect contrast that the Latin foregrounds.

The cantharidas / sutorio atramento suicide-or-ink ambiguity in Fam. 9.21.4. cantharidas sumpsisse (``took cantharides,'' a poisonous beetle) and sutorio atramento absolutus (``acquitted by shoemaker's blacking'') are two interlocked obscurities in Cicero's antiquarian Papisius/Carbo joke. The standard reading is that both are suicide-by-poison idioms (with the ``ink'' of shoemaker's atramentum standing for the poison). The translation preserves the literal images with the obscurity intact rather than disambiguating in body; a translator-notes entry should record the choice.

The eulogon doubling across Att. 13.05 and 13.07. eulogon ``reasonable'' / ``stands to reason'' appears in two consecutive letters of the Tusculanum philosophical-writing cluster, both times as a Stoic dialectical term in parenthesis. Worth flagging as the cluster's stable philosophical-technical vocabulary item.

The frequentia / celebritas distinction in Att. 12.40.3. The two words look like synonyms but are kept deliberately distinct across the cluster: celebritas ``frequented spot'' is the fanum-cluster keyword (the positive value Cicero now wants for the shrine site, the reversal of 12.13--12.16's solitudo-love); frequentia ``crowd of visitors'' is the negative thing he flees on his estates. Worth a corpus-level note as the cluster's most precise lexical choice.

eng: a place to grow old in
note: The engerama broadening in Att. 12.44.2. The Greek term first appeared in 12.29.2 (Atticus suggests Cicero buy a property as ``something to grow old in'') and was paired there with entaphion ``something to be buried in.'' Here in 12.44 the term broadens from a specific property-purchase to the conceptual goal of the whole project: not just a shrine but a place to grow old in. Worth flagging as the moment the project takes on its later, broader character.

eng: I have beaten down my spirit
alternatives: vincam animum (I shall master my mind)
note: The contundo / vincam / exculto philosophical-discipline register, Att. 12.44.3--12.46.1. The brutal physical metaphor contundo animum ``I have beaten down my spirit'' (12.44.3) is picked up and softened to vincam animum ``I shall master my mind'' (12.46.1), and then sublimated into the cultivated-mind formula exculto enim animo nihil agreste, nihil inhumanum est (12.46.1, near-Terentian). The three-step progression --- pound, master, cultivate --- is the cluster's clearest philosophical-discipline arc. Worth a corpus-level note.

The o tempora! rendering at Att. 12.49.2. Rendered ``What times!'' deliberately fresh rather than ``O the times!'' to avoid echoing the famous Catilinarian o tempora, o mores. The principle of fresh-rendering of canonical lines (STYLE.md ``Things to avoid'' §3) applies even to Cicero quoting himself --- arguably especially. Worth a translator-notes entry as a methodological example.

eng: I will be damned if I am not
note: The Cassius voice register in Fam. 15.19. Shorter clauses, fewer suspensions, colloquial oaths preserved at full force (peream nisi sollicitus sum ``I will be damned if I am not''), denser per-word Greek deployment with technical coinages (the philhedonic/philokaloi/philodikaioi tricolon), military tricolon scis ... scis ... scis. Worth a corpus-level note on non-Cicero correspondent voices: the project's working assumption that they should sound audibly different from Cicero's own voice while remaining clean modern English.

lat: ouk estin hedeos aneu tou kalos kai dikaios zen
eng: there is no living pleasantly without living finely and justly
note: The canonical Epicurus quotation in Fam. 15.19. ouk estin hedeos aneu tou kalos kai dikaios zen ``there is no living pleasantly without living finely and justly'' --- a verbatim Epicurus citation by Cassius. Worth a corpus-level allusion entry and a glossary note when the apparatus pass reaches Fam. 15.

The mock-juridical vi hominibus armatis in Fam. 15.16.3. The all-caps quotation of the praetor's interdict (Gaius 4.155) preserved in small-caps Latin rather than rendered in mock-archaic English (``by armed men with violence'' or the like). Worth a translator-notes entry on the choice not to archaize legal formulae when their Latin form is the joke.

lat: maerorem minui, dolorem nec potui nec, si possem, vellem
eng: my mourning I have lessened; my grief I could not lessen, nor would I, if I could
note: The maeror/dolor distinction in Att. 12.28.2. maerorem minui, dolorem nec potui nec, si possem, vellem is the canonical Ciceronian split between maeror (public mourning, the kind one performs and can modulate) and dolor (inward grief, which one cannot reduce by an act of will and would not, even if one could). The rendering choice between ``mourning''/``grief'' vs the more uniform ``grief''/``pain'' shapes the whole Astura cluster. Worth a corpus-level note.

eng: thing to grow old in / thing to be buried in
note: The engerama/entaphion pair in Att. 12.29.2. Atticus suggests Cicero purchase a property as something to grow old in (engerama); Cicero replies that what he wants is something to be buried in (entaphion). The two Greek words are the most-quoted moment of the four-letter sub-batch; rendered as plain English glosses (``thing to grow old in'' / ``thing to be buried in'') rather than something more literary, on the principle that the diminutive force of the Greek terms lands in their literal ordinariness.

lat: secundum te nihil est mihi amicius solitudine
eng: after you, nothing is more friendly to me than solitude
alternatives: sequor celebritatem (what I am after is a frequented spot)
celebritatem requiro (I am after a frequented spot)
note: The solitudo-to-celebritas reversal across Att. 12.13--12.37. Cicero's opening position is the famous secundum te nihil est mihi amicius solitudine (12.15.1); by 12.27.1 the position has reversed into sequor celebritatem ``what I am after is a frequented spot,'' and by 12.37.2 celebritatem requiro ``I am after a frequented spot'' has become the engine of the fanum project. The reversal is the cluster's central arc and worth a corpus-level note on how the shrine project re-orients the grief.

lat: feci quod profecto ante me nemo, ut ipse me per litteras consolarer
eng: I have done what surely no man has done before me, console myself by writing
note: The Consolatio self-reference in Att. 12.14.3. feci quod profecto ante me nemo, ut ipse me per litteras consolarer is the cluster's clearest naming of the now-lost Consolatio as a self-addressed literary act. With 12.20.3 (de luctu minuendo), the two references bracket the composition of the treatise within the March 45 BC retreat. Worth a corpus-level note when the lost Consolatio is reconstructed for the apparatus pass.

lat: unam rem ad me scripsisti; de qua decrevi nihil tibi rescribere
eng: you have written to me about one matter; on which I have determined to write nothing back to you
note: The Publilia question's elliptical refusals in Att. 12.26.2 and the daggered crux of 12.32.2. The unam rem ad me scripsisti; de qua decrevi nihil tibi rescribere of 12.26.2 and the \dag cum Publilio loqueretur \dag crux of 12.32.2 (autograph paragraph) are the cluster's two Publilia moments; both are deliberately elliptical in the Latin and rendered without expansion in the English. Worth a translator-notes entry on the cluster's discretion around the marriage's dissolution.

lat: servio cupiditati et dolori meo ut a te regi velim
eng: I am the slave of my own longing and grief, and so I want to be steered by you
note: The cupiditas/dolor pairing in Att. 12.31.2. servio cupiditati et dolori meo ut a te regi velim (``I am the slave of my own longing and grief, and so I want to be steered by you'') is the cluster's most personal admission of dependence on Atticus; the cupiditas here is the longing for the shrine, not for remarriage. Worth a corpus-level note on cupiditas under grief, distinct from cupiditas under desire.

alternatives: oppidum cadavera (so many corpses of towns lie cast down together)
unius mulierculae animula (the little life-breath of one little woman)
note: The Servius / Cicero consolation pair (Fam. 4.5 + 4.6) as load-bearing pair. These two letters are among the most-read pieces of Latin prose Cicero wrote, and the pair is one of the great consolation-correspondences of Latin literature. The headnotes cross-link them explicitly. The homunculi / oppidum cadavera / mulierculae animula renderings in 4.5 and the ea me solacia deficiunt / recrudescunt / periphrastic-Caesar renderings in 4.6 should be preserved across any future revision. Worth a corpus-level note on the voice-distinction between Servius's Stoic-jurist register and Cicero's grieving-philosopher register in the pair.

lat: tempori cedere, id est necessitati parere, semper sapientis est habitum
eng: to give way to the times --- that is, to obey necessity --- has always been held the wise man's part
note: The Marcellus exile cluster as the Pro Marcello's epistolary companion. Fam. 4.7--4.11 bracket the September 46 BC speech; the headnotes position them explicitly. The Stoic commonplace tempori cedere in 4.9 is the engine of the speech and worth a cross-reference in any future apparatus pass.

The Caecina exile cluster as the substantive policy-letter cluster. Fam. 6.6 is Cicero's most candid public-private overlap on Caesar's likely course in late 46 BC, and the cluster (6.5 / 6.6 / 6.7 / 6.8) sits between the Marcellus and Ligarius letters in the bound-volume narrative of mercy correspondence. Caecina's reply in 6.7 --- the cluster's one inward-facing voice --- preserves a deliberately learned-equal-under-unequal-fortune register.

The salutation correction for Fam. 4.10 (Cicero -> Marcellus, not Marcellus -> Cicero). The launch brief misidentified the sender; the Latin salutation CICERO MARCELLO S. is unambiguous. The post-recall content (Theophilus setting out, anticipating Marcellus's return, the wry tarda decessio) confirms. Worth a translator-notes entry as a guide for any future re-pass of the cluster.

The Pro Deiotaro dating peg for Fam. 9.12. The letter's internal oratiuncula pro Deiotaro reference is the chronological anchor; the speech itself dates to November 45 BC, so 9.12 must postdate. Worth a methodological note on preferring internal references to placeholder month/year metadata.

The Caesarian intercalary calendar continues to shape late-46 BC datelines (Fam. 13.77, 6.1). 13.77 sits in the first intercalary month (~ mid-November); 6.1 sits at the end of the second intercalary month (~ mid-February of the unreformed calendar). The session-13 note on the calendar should be extended.

lat: non agendi aliquid, sed illius concessu et beneficio quiescendi
eng: not for any action of our own, but for keeping quietly, by his concession and favour
note: The quiescere register under autocracy in Fam. 4.6. Cicero's non agendi aliquid, sed illius concessu et beneficio quiescendi ``not for any action of our own, but for keeping quietly, by his concession and favour'' is the cleanest statement in the corpus of the political register of quiescere under Caesarian clemency. The verb will recur; worth a corpus-level note.

alternatives: tou poietou e parembeblemenoi (the poet's own or interpolated)
note: The Alexandrian-critic conceit in Fam. 9.10. Cicero applies obelizing-and-interpolation language to a money dispute as a sustained metaphor; the three Greek phrases obelizei, tou poietou e parembeblemenoi, and sumbioten all belong to the same conceit. Worth a translator-notes entry on the metaphor's coherence across the letter.

Single-section Latin source files this session. Fam. 13.30, 13.31, 13.52, 13.67, 13.70 [drafted last session], 13.79 are all single-section in Perseus's TEI. The English supplies \ciceroSection{1} markers for parallel-JSON consistency where appropriate.

alternatives: non vulgaris esse commendationem hanc meam
note: The non vulgaris tier-marker as recognised genre signal across the recommendation books. Fam. 13.35 (Philoxenus / Novum Comum), 13.36 (Demetrius Megas), and 13.26 (Lepidus / Mescinius / Mindius) all carry the formula in some variant (non vulgaris esse commendationem hanc meam, maiore studio neminem commendarim, quasi commendaticias). The formula explicitly out-ranks the routine commendaticia tier and signals to the recipient that the present letter is to be read with extra weight. The trio of session-13 occurrences plus prior session attestations would support a backmatter glossary stub on the non vulgaris formula as a recognised letter-of-recommendation tier-marker.

lat: a. d. v K. intercal. priores a. 708 (46)
note: The Caesarian intercalary calendar in Fam. 6.14. The dateline a. d. v K. intercal. priores a. 708 (46) refers to the first of the two intercalary months Caesar inserted in 46 BC before the Julian reform took effect on 1 January 45 BC; the date corresponds roughly to 26 November 46 BC in the unreformed Roman calendar. This is the calendar that ends with Caesar's reform; Fam. 6.14 sits inside that transitional intercalary period. Worth a glossary entry on the 46 BC intercalary calendar for the scholar profile.

lat: ut si a me manumissus esset
eng: as if he had been freed by my own hand
note: The ut si a me manumissus esset formula in the Servius cluster. Fam. 13.21 (Hammonius) and 13.23 (Anchialus) both deploy the ``as if he had been freed by my own hand'' fiction --- a recurring Ciceronian commendation move that casts the freedman as Cicero's own client through transitive patronage of the actual patron. Worth a corpus-level note on this patronage formula.

Perseus canonical numbering versus chronological order in Ad Familiares book 9. The Varro/Paetus/Dolabella sequence of book 9 is arranged in Perseus's TEI by manuscript order rather than by date; the actual chronological order is 9.7 (May 46), 9.1 (Dec. 47 / Jan. 46), 9.2 (April 46), 9.3 (April 46), 9.4 (June 46), 9.5 (June 46), 9.6 (June 46), then 9.13 (Dec. 46 / Jan. 45). This is a recurring pattern in the Ad Familiares books and worth a corpus-level methodological note: the file-prefix NNNbc- sort, the date field, and Perseus's numerical order are three distinct orderings that all need to be kept straight.

Iambic senarius embedded in Fam. 9.7. Cicero's quid hic mihi faciet patri? scans as a comic trimeter quotation (or compositional fragment) about the Caesarian killings in Africa. Whether quoted from a contemporary comedy or coined for the moment, the metrical shape is intentional. Worth a translator-notes entry as a candidate prosodic detail for the scholar profile.

alternatives: cum opera nostra patria sive non possit uti sive nolit (since our country either cannot or will not use our service)
note: The post-Pharsalus studia / litterae / libri hardening continues through Fam. 9.7 and 9.6. The note recorded in session 12 on the rhetorical economy of Cicero's literary-otium turn extends: Fam. 9.7's polloi mathetai kreissones didaskalon (``many pupils outdo their teachers'') and 9.6's cum opera nostra patria sive non possit uti sive nolit ``since our country either cannot or will not use our service'' belong to the same rhetorical economy. The voice across Fam. 9.1--9.7 plus 9.13 is extraordinarily coherent.

Single-section Latin source files. Fam. 13.20, 13.25, 13.33, 13.34, 13.37, 13.39, 13.70, 13.71 are all single-section letters in Perseus's TEI. Where appropriate, the English added \ciceroSection{1} markers for parallel-JSON consistency; where the Latin's lack of subdivision was load-bearing (very short notes), it was preserved. Structural-divergence checks should expect a mix of patterns across book 13.

The Caelius voice across Fam. 8.2/8.6/8.7/8.17. Caelius's letters to Cicero are written in a register utterly distinct from Cicero's own --- gossipy, slangy, hurried, friend-to-friend Roman political insider talk, a generation younger and more excitable. The English should keep that voice consistently colloquial-modern across the whole book 8 sequence, not elevate it to Cicero's higher register. The session preserved the consaepta jury-wattle figure in 8.6 without explanation, kept the freezing/hot/superheated Curio chain literal, and resisted glossing the Dolabella-Tullia warning. Cross-book voice consistency worth a corpus-level note.

The Diodorus / Chrysippus material in Fam. 9.4. One of the clearest small windows onto Hellenistic modal logic in surviving classical Latin. The Master Argument of Diodorus Cronus (only past possibilities are possibilities, only what will happen is possible) contested by Chrysippus's broader definition of kata Chrysippon dynaton. May merit a backmatter glossary stub linking to the contemporaneous De Fato.
The architectos / fabros figure in Fam. 9.2 §5 anticipates the rhetorical-political imagery of the Brutus (early 46 BC) and could be cross-linked once that work is drafted.

lat: ignoscunt mihi, revocant in consuetudinem pristinam
note: The studia / litterae / libri hardening across Fam. 9.1, 9.2, 9.3. These nouns shift in pressure across the three letters from ``leisure pursuits'' to something nearer ``the only ground left to live on.'' The English in 9.1 (ignoscunt mihi, revocant in consuetudinem pristinam), 9.2 (una vivere in studiis nostris), and 9.3 (the medical metaphor) lets the pressure build without spelling it out. Worth a corpus-level note on the rhetorical economy of Cicero's literary-otium turn under the dictatorship.

Fam. 16.20 single-section structure. Perseus presents the Tiro convalescence letter as a single undivided paragraph; the English supplies a \ciceroSection{1} marker for parallel-JSON consistency. Two other letters this session (Fam. 9.3, 9.4) have the same single-section structure in Perseus and received the same treatment. Structural-divergence checks should expect these.

The Bursa hatred / Caelius-Curio reproach pairing. Cicero's Fam. 7.2 of February 51 BC (the Bursa-conviction letter, already drafted) says he hated Bursa worse than Clodius himself ``because the latter at least had a great public motive.'' The Caelius letter Fam. 8.17 of February 48 BC enacts a similar private-grief / private-reproach register in the opposite direction (Caelius now reproaching Cicero and Curio). Across the book-7-to-book-8 sequence the two voices form a diptych on private bitterness toward political collaborators. Worth flagging as a cross-reference for the scholar profile.

Fam. 15.13 salutation-vs.-back-reference puzzle. Perseus dates 15.13 to the same time as 15.4 and 15.10 (both late 51 BC at Tarsus), but the salutation L. PAVLLO COS. (without desig.) requires a date on or after 1 January 50 BC when Paullus took office. The companion 15.12 to L. PAVLLO COS. DESIG. correctly belongs to autumn 51 (Paullus had just been elected). The two letters are thus three or four months apart, not contemporaneous; Perseus's geographic ``same place'' back-reference is right (both from the Cilician winter quarters) but the temporal one is loose. Session chose to trust the salutation. Worth a footnote in any future chronological-table apparatus.

Worker reports noted that Fam. 8.2's opening repraesentante is daggered in Perseus, and that Fam. 8.17 carries three further corruptions. The post-Pharsalus textual transmission of book 8 is rougher than book 9; the parallel JSON segments for 8.2, 8.7, and 8.17 carry the daggered passages in their notes field.

The orphan Latin file latin/letters/054bc-ad-familiares-13-49.tex (flagged by validate.py) remains in the tree as a known artifact of the Phase-0 session 1 fetch debugging. The handoff script has a one-off rule to remove this specific file; the warning is harmless.

Fam. 7.32 sustained legal-metaphor. The mock-property-law conceit possessio salinarum mearum (``title to my saltworks'') is sustained through the interdicta (praetorian injunctions on property) deployed as the imagined apparatus for defending Cicero's copyright on his own jokes, with seven Greek wit-category tags supplying the metalanguage of jest theory. Worth a corpus-level note as one of the densest legal-jest passages outside the speeches.

alternatives: atar poieseis (but yet you will write) [Manutius's conjecture]
note: Q. fr. 3.5 corrupt §4 \dag AMPOEIS \dag. The Greek manuscript reading is meaningless and editorial conjectures (e.g. Manutius's atar poieseis ``but yet you will write'') are speculative. Rendered ``[corrupt]'' in the body; flagged in the JSON.

Q. fr. 3.5 §6 \dag crebrius \dag and \dag adiurat, debere tibi valde renuntiant \dag. Two further corruptions in the closing accounts passage; sense reconstructed loosely; both rendered ``[corrupt]'' in the body.

Sallustius the literary advisor across Q. fr. 3.4 §1 and 3.5 §1. The same future-historian young man (Cicero's literary interlocutor at Tusculum in late 54) urges Cicero to prosecute Gabinius (3.4) and prompts the rewrite of De Republica (3.5). Worth an entities registry entry on the next enrichment pass.

The Dolabella-suppression discipline across Fam. 3.10--3.13. Through the four Appius letters Dolabella is never named: ille adulescens of 3.10 §5, the unnamed prosecutor of 3.11, the unnamed husband-of-Tullia of 3.12. The translator should resist any urge to gloss in the body; the suppression is the rhetorical event.
The censor, ut spero running gag across Fam. 3.10--3.13. Cicero opens, closes, and salutes Appius as ``censor, I hope''; preserved across the four letters as a structural grace note.

Att. 5.21 Brutus-Salaminian case is load-bearing for the later Ad Brutum. The 48% compound interest, the two 56 BC senatusconsulta under Lentulus and Philippus, the lex Gabinia, Cicero's 12% edict with annual compounding, the withdrawal of the cavalry, the refusal to give Scaptius a prefecture, and the Salaminians arriving with the money --- this episode will be referenced obliquely in the run-up to the assassination and in Ad Brutum of 43 BC. When those letters come up, the headnotes should cross-reference Att. 5.21 §§ 10--13.

Att. 5.21 §7 Cyprus 200 talents. Cicero refused the 200 Attic talents the Cyprians had customarily paid to keep troops out of winter quarters --- the same community (Salaminians) and suggestively close to the 200-talent settlement Scaptius later demanded; worth a possible cross-reference flag.

Cicero's Cilician moral self-portrait. The recurring boast in this batch --- 12% edict, no statues, no shrines, no chariots, the famine handled by exhortation --- is the moral self-portrait Cicero is consciously building for posterity. Headnotes for the Cilician period should be consistent in flagging this as performance-for-Atticus, not naive self-praise.

Fam. 2.18 and 13-57 works.yaml description swap. The two Thermus letters' notes: or description fields appear to have been swapped: 2.18's description in works.yaml refers to ``M. Anneius leave'' but the actual content is on Thermus's choice of interim governor; 13-57's description refers to ``Curius at Patrae'' but the actual content is on Thermus releasing M. Anneius back to Cicero. The PM or a future metadata-cleanup pass should sanity-check works.yaml title_english and notes fields for these two entries against the Latin.

Fam. 2.17 misaligned description. The works.yaml entry's general description (``handover of imperium to Caelius Caldus the inexperienced Cilician quaestor heir'') matches what Fam. 2.15 §4 actually is, not what 2.17 is. 2.17 is to a proquaestor (Caelius Caldus serving with Bibulus in Syria) on six topics including the 333,000-drachma quaestorial account. PM should review the works.yaml description for 2.17 against the Latin.

lat: Pollon aristeuein kai hypeirochon emmenai allon
eng: always to be best and to outstrip the others
note: Recurring Homeric register in the autumn 54 BC Q. fr. letters. 3.5 has Iliad 6.208 (Glaucus's father's command, ``always to be best''), 3.7 has Iliad 16.385--388 (Zeus's autumn rains and the crooked-judging elders). Worth flagging for the eventual greek-phrases.json enrichment pass as the heightened private-mood self-quotation register of the Tusculum days.

eng: if you are well, it is well; I am well
note: Cross-letter (Fam. 14 Brundisium-period register). The set of 14 Brundisium-period Terentia letters (14.8--14.13, 14.15--14.17, 14.19, 14.21--14.24) all open with the conventional Roman epistolary formula S. v. b. e. v. (= si vales bene est, ego valeo, ``if you are well, it is well; I am well'') or its scribal variant S. v. h. e. v. The English consistently renders all variants as ``If you are well, it is well; I am well.'' The exception is Fam. 14.20 of 1 October 47, written en route from Brundisium to Tusculum after the Caesar-meeting, which drops the formula --- Cicero is in motion now and the practical domestic register has resumed. The dropping of the formula at 14.20 is a load-bearing stylistic signal worth a corpus-level note.

Fam. 14.17 corrupt dateline. circ. xl i K. Ian. in Perseus is not a valid Roman date; xl cannot appear in an a. d. count (maximum gap is xix K.). The single-character corruption xl i -> xi = 22 December is the likeliest emendation; xvi = 17 December is also defensible. Adopted 22 December with month precision pending a Shackleton Bailey check.

eng: it came about through our own negligence
alternatives: summa culpa mea / nullo ipsius delicto
note: Fam. 14.11 wife-side softening. nostra factum esse neglegentia ``it came about through our own negligence'' is the wife-side softening of the same week's Att. 11.17 admission to Atticus summa culpa mea / nullo ipsius delicto. To Atticus the fault is Cicero's alone; to Terentia it is jointly ``ours.'' Worth flagging both as a register contrast and as evidence for Cicero's same-week multi-correspondent practice.

Fam. 14.13 nuntium remittere. The technical Roman divorce formula, rendered ``sending the notice of divorce.'' First substantive appearance in the Brundisium-period Terentia letters of the Tullia/Dolabella divorce theme that runs in parallel with Att. 11.23/11.25 of the same months.
Fam. 14.20 labrum = tub. The bathing basin in a Roman bath suite, rendered ``tub'' rather than ``basin'' for the domestic register.

Salutation OCR scanno in Fam. 14.10 Latin source. latin/letters/047bc-ad-familiares-14-10.tex carries TERENTIAE 5VAE (the 5 an OCR misread of S); silently corrected in the English \ciceroLetterOpener{...} to SVAE. The Latin source file retains the scanno for a future fetch-cleanup pass.
Fam. 14.19 recipient: field typo in works.yaml. TVLLIVS TEREM'IAE SVAE should be TVLLIVS TERENTIAE SVAE. Worth normalizing during the next metadata-cleanup pass.

Fam. 14.6 salutation SVIS S. D. Bare ``to his own, greetings,'' no addressee named. Plural verbs in the body (videatis, scitis) confirm collective family address; unusual register for Book 14, worth an entities-mentions tag for the household.

eng: send Pollex packing as soon as you can
note: Fam. 14.6 Pollex the courier. Pollex (Pollicem ... extrudas ``send Pollex packing as soon as you can'') recurs as a household courier through this period; worth an entities-mentions tag.
Volumnia in Fam. 14.16. Almost certainly Volumnia Cytheris, the actress and mistress of M. Antonius; worth an entity registry entry on the next enrichment pass.

lat: is quicum sumus / qui eas dispensavit
eng: the man I am with / the one who has been managing them
note: Cross-letter (Book 11 motif). The book systematically declines to name either Pompey (is quicum sumus 'the man I am with') or Philotimus (qui eas dispensavit 'the one who has been managing them'). The English follows the Latin in keeping these elliptical; identifications go in the headnotes only. This is a load-bearing stylistic decision for the entire book and worth a single corpus-level translator-notes entry.

lat: cistophoro in Asia ... ad sestertium bis et viciens
eng: in cistophori, to the amount of twenty-two hundred thousand sesterces
note: Kept the Latin coin name (the cistophorus, the silver tetradrachm of the Attalid mints continued in proconsular Asia).

eng: formally entered upon the inheritance under the terms of the will
note: Rendered to preserve the technical Roman ritual of cretio.

lat: recipio tempore me domo te nunc
note: Two daggered cruxes (non and recipio tempore me domo te nunc) preserved verbatim with conjectural surface renderings; the second is heavily corrupt and editorial resolution is contested.
The closing line rendered as a flat hard close to preserve its terminal force after the long deliberation.

eng: Would that I had never been taken up at birth
alternatives: Would that I had never been born
note: Rendered to preserve the technical Roman ritual sense of suscipere (the father's lifting of a newborn for acknowledgement), rather than the easier 'Would that I had never been born.'
Daggered crux preserved; rendering 'of goodwill' is conjectural from the corruption.
Verbatim self-quotation from Cicero's earlier letter to Caesar preserved in English quotation marks; this is a rare structural feature worth flagging to the apparatus.

lat: ibi facile est, quod quale sit hic gravius existimare
note: Two daggered cruxes (et advideo tamen exspecto and ibi facile est, quod quale sit hic gravius existimare) preserved; second one is doubtful and worth a translator-notes entry.
Prefatory Perseus dateline corrupt as iii Nou. luot. resolved to iii Non. Iun. (3 June 47) via clean internal colophon at end of section 5; date correction recorded.
Rendered for the technical formal audience-of-recognition before Caesar (rather than the loose 'his attention' or 'his cognizance'); worth a glossary entry.

lat: Sullana confers; ... moderatione paulo minus temperata
note: The deliberately bitter litotes preserved without softening into the easier 'less restrained' because the original is built on the polite-form irony of moderatione ... temperata.
Daggered crux preserved verbatim; standard editorial reading is 'the other course' (i.e., Caesar going direct Patrae-to-Sicily) but the corruption is acknowledged in the headnote.
Daggered crux in oppidum ponat preserved.
Daggered cruxes (ad me visat in section 1 and querere in section 2) preserved with conservative renderings; the section-3 sums HS XII / CCI(supset)(supset) rendered as '12,000' / '10,000' sesterces.

lat: apud epistulas velim ut possim adversas / huius miserrimae facultate confectus conflictor
note: Two adjacent badly-corrupt daggered passages preserved verbatim with conjectural surface renderings; both worth translator-notes entries.
The Iliad 22.304-305 Hector quotation flagged for the allusions pass; the framing of the elder Sextus Peducaeus's 63 BC advice with a hexameter quotation is preserved without smoothing.
The dilemma is a topos of political philosophy worth flagging as a possible Stoic-school allusion.
The Greek proverb (= the impossible) preserved as an in-text English gloss; worth a dedicated entry in data/greek-phrases.json since an English-only rendering would lose the proverb.

lat: regnandi contentio est, in qua pulsus est modestior rex et probior et integrior
eng: more modest, more upright, more sound
note: Tricolon rendered with deliberate threefold 'more modest, more upright, more sound' to preserve the figure --- and Cicero's striking application of rex to Pompey himself flagged for apparatus.

lat: Regnum non modo Romano homini sed ne Persae quidem cuiquam tolerabile
eng: a kingship not merely intolerable to a Roman --- intolerable to any Persian
note: The two Thucydidean tags on Themistoclean foresight (Thuc. 1.138) preserved verbatim in Greek brackets with English glosses; the first tag is truncated in the transmitted text after di' and a standard continuation was supplied. Latin tag rendered to preserve the pointed contrast.

eng: mark what I am telling you
note: The Pindar fr. 105 Snell-Maehler tag recurs as Cicero's recurring code-phrase to Atticus for 'read between the lines' --- worth a corpus-level allusion note pointing at Pindar fr. 105 and listing all the Atticum occurrences.
The Peripatetic pair is technical character-formation vocabulary about Cicero's nephew --- worth a translator-note on Cicero's use of Greek philosophical idiom for moral judgement on young Quintus.
The manuscript crux preserved as [Greek: EKITAONON] with daggers, unresolved.

The works.yaml date -0049-05-15 was a placeholder that conflicted with the Perseus dateline (transmitted corruptly as viq i Id. Mai. but reading as viii Id. Mai. = 8 May per Shackleton Bailey); corrected to -0049-05-08 per the chronology between 10.13 (7 May) and 10.15 (12 May).

lat: tabellarius si apud te esse quas satis fecissest dares
note: The corrupt passage preserved between daggers; standard Shackleton-Bailey-style emendations not silently incorporated.
Rendered 'warrant' rather than 'passport' since the document is specifically the imperial travel-warrant authorising use of the cursus publicus.

The proverbial 'eavesdroppers of Corycus' kept as an inline gloss to preserve the proverb's piratical flavour; conficior rendered 'I am being torn apart' over 'I am undone' to preserve the violent register of Cicero's collapse.

Perseus dateline 'anno incerto fort. 691' (= conjecturally 63 BC) reported in the headnote and sidecar notes, but the entry date in works.yaml left at the year-range placeholder -0054-07-01 pending a separate chronological-refinement pass on Book 13.

Scinio read as Scipio (Q. Caecilius Metellus Pius Scipio, Pompey's father-in-law); the wordplay with vel cum genero honeste (= with his son-in-law, i.e. with Pompey) makes Scipio the obvious referent. Flag for apparatus.

The very short letter preserved as just the opening fragment; the longer Att. 9.2A 'Postridie Idus Martias...' is not in the Perseus TEI file (and is not a separate works.yaml entry); modern editions split what some manuscripts run together as 9.2 + 9.2A. The translation here covers only 9.2 proper.

lat: euelabeteon ton katalyonta me autos airetai
eng: be on guard against the destroyer, lest he himself be raised up [as the new tyrant]
note: The nine-thesis theseis list: rendering policy for the Perseus beta-coded Greek (grave-marks, parenthetical breathings) was to silently normalise to transliteration in the English while preserving the Perseus transmission verbatim in the parallel-sidecar Latin segment.
